reports.title: U.S. Semiconductors & Semiconductor Capital Equipment: Shifting Ratings Ahead of CQ1
reports.companies_covered: WDC, STX, SWKS, QRVO, QCOM, PENG
reports.headline: Barclays keeps WDC at OW and raises PT to $405 on a materially more bullish HDD pricing/ASP outlook and tighter LT HDD supply-demand.
reports.summary: Barclays’ sector note shifts several ratings ahead of CQ1, but for WDC the key change is a PT increase to $405 from $325 while maintaining OW. The thesis is that HDD pricing has further legs as Flash pricing rises, supply discipline holds, and hyperscaler demand visibility extends into CY27-28. Barclays materially lifts industry HDD rev/ASP assumptions and raises WDC EPS ests, arguing the market is underappreciating a more durable structural re-rating in drives rather than a short cyclical spike.
important_observations: Barclays sees drives as the most attractive value pocket in semis, with more pricing leverage left in HDD vs Flash.
The firm argues HDD industry re-rating could be more permanent given duopoly structure and capex/supply discipline.
WDC is positioned to benefit from higher-capacity mix, stronger Mass Capacity pricing, and a structurally tighter Nearline market.
Barclays frames ASP, not units, as the main earnings driver in CY26-27, implying margin and EPS torque can remain strong even with unit declines.
The note suggests upside risk to current HDD pricing assumptions as LTAs roll off and vendors renegotiate at materially higher levels.
Barclays still views WDC favorably even while noting STX’s 40TB/HAMR transition may improve STX’s relative cost position over time.
new_data: Barclays raises CY26 HDD industry rev growth to +31% y/y from +14% prior.
CY26 HDD rev forecast lifted to $30B from $25B prior; CY27 lifted to $39B from $30B prior.
CY26 HDD units now seen at 121M vs 110M prior; CY27 at 119M vs 107M prior.
CY26 ASP forecast raised to +33% y/y from +27% prior; CY27 to +34% from +20% prior.
Barclays models Mass Capacity $/TB up +7% y/y in CY26 and +15% in CY27.
For WDC specifically, Barclays now forecasts rev growth of +39% in CY26 and +32% in CY27 vs prior +25% and +26%.
management_insights: Industry commentary is said to point to mid-high single-digit $/TB increases through each qtr of CY26, with Barclays seeing further upside.
Mgmt commentary cited by Barclays suggests both HDD vendors remain committed to not adding capacity.
Nearline capacity is described as allocated through CY26 and into CY27, with cloud customers already discussing CY28 demand.
WDC downside risk is explicitly tied to a worse-than-expected pricing environment, underscoring pricing as the core driver.

estimate_change_details.note: PT raised on higher CY27 EPS and a higher target multiple.
Barclays FY1 EPS estimate increased.
Barclays FY2 EPS estimate increased.
Rounded from $25.4B to $29.7B; industry HDD forecast, not WDC-only revenue.
Rounded from $29.6B to $38.8B; industry HDD forecast, not WDC-only revenue.

evidence_quality: Primary + analyst model work: Barclays cites company data, prior company commentary, and its own revised HDD model.
Triangulated industry view: combines company commentary, Flash pricing context, IDC data, and Barclays sector work.
Mostly forward-looking/inference-heavy for WDC: PT and est changes rely on Barclays assumptions for pricing, mix, and supply discipline.
Limited new primary WDC-specific disclosure in this note; conviction depends more on industry structure and pricing analysis than fresh mgmt engagement.
risks_or_watch_items: HDD pricing could underwhelm if supply/demand tightness eases faster than Barclays expects.
Flash pricing could normalize, reducing relative HDD pricing umbrella and weakening the bullish storage thesis.
STX’s HAMR/40TB execution could shift competitive dynamics and relative margin leadership over time.
Watch hyperscaler demand follow-through into CY27-28 and whether allocation/tightness persists after current LTAs roll.
Any deviation from capital discipline or capacity additions by HDD vendors would challenge the structural re-rating thesis.
key_numbers: WDC rating: OW maintained.
WDC PT: $405 vs $325 prior.
WDC share price in report: $383.81 on 21-Apr-2026.
WDC EPS: FY1 9.19 vs 8.68 prior; FY2 13.93 vs 12.42 prior.
WDC PT methodology: 24x CY27 EPS of $16.83 vs prior 22x on $14.74.
Barclays WDC rev growth fcst: +39% CY26, +32% CY27.
HDD industry rev fcst: $29.7B CY26 and $38.8B CY27.
HDD units fcst: 121.4M CY26 and 118.9M CY27.
Industry ASP fcst: +33% y/y CY26 and +34% y/y CY27.
Mass Capacity $/TB fcst: +7% y/y CY26 and +15% y/y CY27.

reports.title: HDD Demand (and Pricing) Still Strengthening; Moving STX to Top Pick
reports.headline: MS keeps OW on STX/WDC, moves Top Pick to STX, lifts PTs and ests on stronger HDD demand, tighter supply, and firmer 2027/2028 pricing.
reports.summary: Morgan Stanley argues the nearline HDD cycle is getting stronger, not weaker, with hyperscaler demand, longer customer visibility, and pricing checks implying a tighter supply backdrop through CY28 and balance only by CY29. The note raises STX/WDC ests and PTs, reiterates OW on both, and shifts Top Pick to STX on a modest valuation discount, faster GM expansion from HAMR-led cost-downs, and greater scope for upward EPS revisions versus Street cons.
important_observations: MS says recent checks point to sustained hyperscaler demand strength and firmer pricing into 2027, extending the 'stronger for longer' HDD thesis.
The core variant view is not just better demand but materially better price/TB and cost/TB dynamics, which together drive GM/EPS well above cons.
MS now sees STX/WDC GM running 400-500 bps above cons through CY27, with STX having the wider gap vs Street and therefore more revision upside.
STX becomes Top Pick mainly on relative valuation, faster HAMR-led cost-downs, and less dilution risk vs WDC, not because MS turned less constructive on WDC.
The note frames HDDs as a more attractive AI infra bottleneck than many peers given 80-90% DC rev exposure, oligopolistic structure, and no greenfield capacity growth.
MS argues the market is over-applying memory-cycle concerns to HDDs despite a tighter industry structure, lower capex intensity, and less risk of supply response.
new_data: Latest channel/check work suggests 2027/2028 HDD negotiations are occurring near $20/TB, well above MS base-case assumptions of $13-15/TB.
MS supply/demand tracker now implies HDD shortages through CY28 and parity only in CY29, one year later than in its original thesis.
New bottom-up cost-per-TB model indicates cost-downs should accel over the next ~6 qtrs as 40TB+ drives ramp, supporting mid/high-50s GMs entering CY27.
MS says industry output may be LSD-MSD% above prior expectations, but demand has improved enough that shortages still widen.
The report introduces a detailed quarterly mix model for STX and WDC, with more explicit assumptions around HAMR/ePMR ramps and high-capacity mix.
management_insights: Mgmt commentary and company discussions are cited to support HAMR/ePMR roadmaps, qualification timing, and high-capacity mix assumptions.
STX mgmt milestones imply HAMR could reach ~80% of nearline EBs by end-FY28 in MS's framework.
WDC public roadmap points to ~60TB ePMR by 2028 and potential 100TB capacity by decade-end; MS models remain more conservative than this.
Company conversations reportedly showed no signs of current HDD cycle weakness and no near-term operational impact from helium risk.

estimate_change_details.note: PT raised; STX moved to Top Pick.
PT raised; OW reiterated.
Higher price/TB assumptions and stronger margin setup.
Raised pricing/cost assumptions were offset by higher diluted shares; report text still frames the change as modestly higher vs prior.
Higher price/TB and stronger mix assumptions.
Rounded from $36.74 to $38.07.
Modest uplift from higher price/TB.
Rounded from $16.35 to $16.70; driven by higher price/TB, lower cost/TB, and less dilution.
Modest uplift on improved HDD assumptions.

evidence_quality: Primary + semi-primary: company-reported data/guidance, public roadmaps, and direct conversations with STX/WDC mgmt.
Triangulated but partly anecdotal: recent HDD channel checks and hyperscaler pricing discussions are central to the thesis.
Heavy analyst model work: supply/demand tracker and bottom-up cost-per-TB / mix models drive much of the upside case.
Useful but not fully verifiable from outside: pricing negotiations and qualification timing rely on checks and inference, so conviction should be high but not treated as hard reported fact.
risks_or_watch_items: Hyperscalers improve HDD utilization/efficiency, reducing demand growth.
Helium supply disruptions constrain HDD output.
HAMR/ePMR roadmap or customer qualification delays.
Unexpected unit capacity additions weaken pricing discipline.
Toshiba or new entrants disrupt the oligopoly.
AI/cloud capex slows on ROIC/FCF scrutiny.
eSSD share gains outpace assumptions and pressure HDD demand.
key_numbers: STX PT $582 from $468; WDC PT $380 from $369.
MS reiterates OW on both; Top Pick shifts from WDC to STX.
2027/2028 pricing checks near $0.02/GB vs MS base case $0.013-0.015/GB.
MS sees HDD shortages of ~200EB in CY26 and ~250EB in CY27.
MS now expects supply/demand parity only in CY29.
STX CY27 EPS base/bull: $32.31 / $41.90; WDC CY27 EPS base/bull: $21.12 / $27.34.
MS says STX/WDC trade at ~13-14x new CY27 EPS, while its FY27 EPS sits 25-30% above cons and FY28 EPS 45-50% above Street.
Base-case CY27 GM: STX 57.9%, WDC 59.0%; bull-case CY27 GM: STX 64.6%, WDC 64.8%.

reports.title: Seagate: Potential Upside to Estimates Remains Despite Recent Robust Increases; Cloud Demand and Pricing Tailwinds to Accelerate Margins; Initiate at OW
reports.headline: JPM init STX at OW with $525 Dec-26 PT on view that AI/cloud storage demand, HDD pricing tailwinds, and HAMR-driven mix can push rev/EPS materially above cons over the next 2-3 yrs.
reports.summary: JPM initiates STX at OW, arguing the stock can still work after a sharp rerating because AI-driven cloud capex, structurally better HDD pricing, and a disciplined duopoly should support a stronger earnings cycle than investors historically associate with HDDs. The note frames STX as a beneficiary of mid-20s exabyte growth, low-single-digit price gains instead of historical price declines, and HAMR-led mix improvement, driving ~25% rev CAGR and 50%+ operating earnings CAGR with upside to cons, especially into CY27/FY27.
important_observations: Core bull case is not just demand growth but a structural change in HDD pricing: rev/EB may turn from historical low-teens declines to flat-to-up/LSD growth.
JPM argues supply discipline by STX/WDC matters as much as AI demand, because higher-capacity drives can meet exabyte growth without flooding unit supply.
Variant view is that investors may still be underestimating how much cloud/AI storage demand can sustain better pricing and higher GP/EB.
HAMR adoption is treated as a key upside lever: faster customer qualification and mix shift could lift exabyte growth and margins beyond base-case assumptions.
The note suggests valuation should be benchmarked vs AI suppliers rather than STX's own pre-AI history, implying further rerating is possible if the capex cycle proves durable.
new_data: Initiation at OW with Dec-26 PT of $525.
JPM forecasts FY25-FY27 rev from $9.1bn to $14.6bn and adj EPS from $8.11 to $20.06.
JPM sees 25% rev CAGR and 50%+ operating earnings CAGR over the medium term.
JPM says STX has qualified a second customer for Mozaic 4 HAMR.
Base case assumes LSD increases in rev/EB vs historical low-teens declines.
JPM expects gross margin to approach 50% by end-CY27 and op margin to reach mid-40s in FY28.
management_insights: Mgmt is presented as experienced in navigating storage cycles, with long-tenured operating and technology leadership.
JPM highlights execution on higher-capacity drive transitions and recent 20%+ y/y exabyte growth as evidence mgmt is converting demand into shipments.
Second customer qualification for Mozaic 4 is cited as a sign HAMR adoption is progressing faster than expected.
Mgmt guidance on HAMR mix appears embedded: 50%+ of shipments in 2H26 and >70% exiting FY27.
estimate_changes: No prior JPM model revision disclosed because this is an initiation.
JPM's framework implies upside vs street CY27/FY27 ests, especially on pricing, margins, and exabyte growth.
PT introduced at $525 based on 22x CY27 EPS.

estimate_change_details.note: New PT introduced on initiation; no prior JPM PT.
Initiation forecast; no prior JPM estimate disclosed.
Initiation forecast; no prior JPM estimate disclosed.
Initiation forecast; no prior JPM estimate disclosed.
Initiation forecast; no prior JPM estimate disclosed.
Initiation forecast; no prior JPM estimate disclosed.
Initiation forecast; no prior JPM estimate disclosed.

Initiation forecast; no prior JPM estimate disclosed.

Initiation forecast; no prior JPM estimate disclosed.

evidence_quality: Primary + analyst model work: uses company-reported results, company slides, and JPM forecasts/valuation.
Partly triangulated with external market/industry data from IDC, Gartner, 650 Group, and Bloomberg.
Some support comes from company-reported/customer qualification datapoints and mgmt roadmap disclosures, which are more concrete than broad thematic AI claims.
A meaningful part of the upside case is analyst inference on pricing durability, capex sustainability, and valuation rerating, so confidence is moderate rather than high-certainty.
No channel checks, survey, or expert-call evidence is cited in the excerpt; thesis leans more on industry structure and model assumptions.
risks_or_watch_items: Cloud capex slowdown could break the demand/pricing setup supporting the bull case.
Capacity constraints could limit STX's ability to fulfill demand even if end-market conditions remain strong.
Faster migration to flash/SSD as NAND density improves or relative TCO narrows is the main technology risk.
Watch whether HDD pricing actually sustains sequential gains rather than reverting to historical deflation.
Track HAMR mix ramp, Mozaic customer adds, and whether exabyte growth stays in the 20%+ range.
key_numbers: Rating/PT: OW, $525 Dec-26 PT
Implied upside: ~39%
FY27E adj EBITDA: $6,132mn
FY27E adj EBIT: $5,809mn
FY26E/FY27E EBIT margin: 33.4% / 39.8%
Medium-term rev CAGR: 25%
Medium-term operating earnings CAGR: 50%+
CY27 EPS used for PT: $23.45
Target multiple: 22x P/E

reports.title: Harnessing the Power of Heat: HDD Versus SSD - Ahead of the Curve Series
reports.headline: TD Cowen argues HAMR keeps HDDs structurally advantaged vs eSSDs in DC storage, with STX the main equity beneficiary and PT raised to $78.
reports.summary: TD Cowen’s industry note argues HAMR is a step-change for nearline HDD economics, enabling materially lower $/TB and potentially higher OEM GMs, which should preserve HDD dominance in DC storage through 2032 despite SSD price declines. The core call is that HDDs remain the lowest-cost medium for the bulk of cold/warm data, SSD/HDD price parity is unlikely this decade if HAMR ramps, and STX is best positioned given its HAMR lead, while WDC should remain competitive with a lagged roadmap and PSTG/LRCX also benefit from separate NAND-related trends.
important_observations: Variant view vs cons is that HDDs and SSDs remain complementary, with ~80% of DC storage still on HDDs by 2032 rather than SSDs displacing HDDs before price parity.
HAMR is framed as a structural margin lever, not just a capacity upgrade, because higher TB/drive lowers BOM per TB and may improve pricing discipline in a consolidated HDD market.
AI is presented as net pos for HDD demand because training/inference ecosystems require large low-cost data lakes even if SSDs serve performance tiers.
SSD share gains in downturns are viewed as self-limiting because NAND capacity additions are highly capex intensive and hyperscalers design storage architectures well ahead of spot-price troughs.
WDC share loss risk is argued to be manageable because customer concentration, deliberate dual-sourcing, and WDC's interim roadmap could blunt STX's HAMR lead.
Non-nearline HDD remains the weak link; the constructive thesis is mainly about nearline/cloud rather than the full HDD portfolio.
new_data: Proprietary model suggests HAMR can be up to 500 bps accretive to HDD OEM GMs.
HAMR yields need to exceed ~40% to be GM-neutral vs current CMR economics.
32TB HAMR is modeled at ~30% lower cost/TB vs 20TB CMR; 36TB HAMR adds another ~10% cost/TB reduction vs 32TB HAMR.
TD Cowen sees HDD cost/TB staying ~4x cheaper than SSDs by decade-end, or ~2x cheaper on TCO after power/space/perf adjustments.
Nearline HDDs are projected to remain ~80% of DC storage by 2032, down from mid-80%s today.
Analyst field work indicates one major CSP could test ~25k HAMR units in CY23 and potentially deploy up to ~2M drives over 2 years if qualified.
management_insights: STX had shipped 32TB HAMR drives to a cloud customer for qualification in Apr-23, with rev ramp expected in early CY24.
STX mgmt's LT GM target is cited at 30-33%; TD Cowen believes HAMR could push STX toward ~34% GM by CY26.
STX CFO commentary indicates HAMR product introductions may occur every 18-20 months vs 10-12 months historically for PMR, implying more complex ramps.
WDC is described as executing well on ePMR/ultraSMR and improving enterprise SSD qualifications after prior controller/firmware issues.
PSTG is portrayed as leveraging DirectFlash/QLC capabilities and seeing selective cloud opportunities, but not as a full nearline HDD replacement across DC storage.

estimate_change_details.note: STX PT raised to $78 from $70.

evidence_quality: Primary/secondary mix: report cites company-reported data, disclosed customer qualification activity, and mgmt commentary, but many conclusions extend beyond what companies explicitly guided.
Triangulated but partly anecdotal: heavy use of analyst field work with CSPs/supply chain to assess SSD/HDD mix, HAMR adoption timing, and customer qualification plans.
Mostly analyst model work on economics: GM uplift, yield thresholds, cost/TB curves, and long-range market sizing are proprietary estimates rather than reported figures.
Industry-data supported: uses market share, capex, and storage demand framing from company data, Gartner, and cloud customer trends.
Weight should be moderate: useful differentiated framework, but key calls depend on HAMR yield/ramp execution and long-dated SSD cost assumptions.
risks_or_watch_items: STX HAMR manufacturing yields and ability to sustain volume production.
Customer qualification timing and whether pilot deployments convert to meaningful purchases.
NAND pricing duration/severity; another deep downcycle could improve SSD competitiveness near term.
Ramp of 30TB+ high-capacity SSDs and QLC/PLC adoption curves.
Cloud/DC capex cadence, as ~70% of nearline HDD demand is tied to new DC builds.
Non-nearline HDD erosion could offset part of the nearline upside across the broader HDD complex.
key_numbers: STX PT to $78 from $70.
HAMR GM uplift potential: up to 500 bps.
HAMR GM-neutral yield threshold: ~40% vs ~85% CMR yield today.
HDDs store ~85% of DC data today; TD Cowen sees ~80% by 2032.
HDD market forecast: $24B by 2032, ~3% CAGR from 2022.
Nearline HDD forecast: ~7% CAGR; exabyte shipments ~18% CAGR.
32TB HAMR cost/TB modeled ~30% below 20TB CMR.
SSD/HDD price ratio seen at ~4x by 2032; TCO ratio below 2x by decade-end.
NAND suppliers would need ~$1.5B WFE to take 1% nearline share from HDDs.
STX nearline share cited at ~45%; projected share gain ~500 bps through CY26.

reports.title: Seagate Technology | North America: Upgrading to Overweight On A More Attractive Multi-Year Earnings Story
reports.headline: MS upg STX to OW from EW and lifts PT to $115 on higher HAMR-driven GM upside and incremental AI-related HDD demand in FY26-27.
reports.summary: Morgan Stanley upgrades STX to OW from EW and raises its PT to $115 from $73, arguing the stock is moving from a cyclical HDD recovery into a structurally stronger earnings phase. The key change is a more bullish bottom-up view on HAMR mix and margin accretion, with LT GM now seen at 32-35% vs mgmt's 30-33% target, plus modest AI-driven HDD exabyte upside in FY26-27. While HAMR mix lowers rev per exabyte and trims rev ests by 1-2%, MS sees the GM lift more than offsetting that pressure, driving 20-30% higher EPS ests and FY27 EPS power above $10.50.
important_observations: Core debate shifts from cyclical recovery timing to whether HAMR can structurally reset STX GM above prior through-cycle levels.
MS argues HAMR is materially margin accretive, with a modeled ~45% normalized GM for 30TB drives and further upside as 40TB/50TB products scale.
AI is framed less as an immediate rev driver and more as an outer-year storage tailwind that benefits HDD alongside flash rather than displacing it outright.
The note implies variant perception vs Street on profitability, with MS 200-300 bps ahead of Street GM in FY25/26 and >10% ahead of Street FY27 EPS power.
Despite lower price/TB and slightly lower rev, MS sees mix/technology leadership creating a stronger earnings algorithm through GM expansion and opex discipline.
Potential catalysts include HAMR customer quals starting as soon as May, broader US tier-1 CSP qualification by CY24-end, share gains, and a future Analyst Day that could lift LT GM guide.
new_data: Rating changed to OW from EW.
PT raised to $115 from $73.
FY24-27 EPS ests raised 18-32%.
FY25-27 GM ests raised by 321-489 bps; FY27 GM now 35.1%.
FY26-27 HDD exabyte fcst raised 4% on AI-related demand assumptions.
HAMR mix now modeled at 8% of HDD exabytes in FY24 and ~50% by FY27.
MS introduces a bottom-up HAMR margin view implying ~45% normalized GM for 30TB HAMR drives.
FY24-27 rev ests trimmed 1-2% due to lower price/EB as HAMR mix rises.
management_insights: Mgmt has indicated any AI-related uplift would be incremental to its mid-20% mass-capacity exabyte CAGR framework.
Mgmt's current LT GM target remains 30-33%, but MS believes this excludes HAMR mix benefits.
Mgmt said the first 1M HAMR shipments should be immediately GM accretive, with margins scaling as more customers qualify.
Mgmt expects to ship 1M HAMR units in C1H24/F2H24, broadly in line with MS assumptions.
Mgmt has said 30% GM can be achieved with ~20% less rev than at peak, supported by cost actions and portfolio changes.
Mgmt has reiterated continued pricing actions and believes opex can remain structurally below pre-COVID levels at similar rev.
estimate_changes: PT increased to $115 from $73 and rating upgraded to OW from EW.
EPS ests increased across FY24-27, led by higher GM assumptions from HAMR mix shift.
GM ests raised 50 bps in FY24 and ~300-500 bps in FY25-27.
Rev ests reduced 1-2% across FY24-27 due to lower HDD price/EB assumptions.
FY26-27 HDD exabyte ests increased 4% on incremental AI demand.

estimate_change_details.note: PT raised as valuation base moves to FY27 EPS and earnings power outlook improves.
Driven by higher GM, partly offset by slightly lower rev.
Benefit from higher HAMR mix and stronger GM outlook.
Higher GM more than offsets lower price/EB.
Outer-year earnings power revised materially higher.
Rounded whole numbers, $M.
Rounded whole numbers, $M.

Change is +489 bps; pct_change not meaningful for margin percentages.

evidence_quality: Primary plus analyst model work: company-reported results/guidance and recent CFO/mgmt meetings are central inputs.
Triangulated with external industry data from IDC, cloud capex work, and Japan channel/industry inputs rather than relying on mgmt alone.
Some AI storage demand support comes from survey data and VAR checks, which are directional and less definitive than reported company data.
HAMR margin conclusions are mostly analyst bottom-up inference/modeling, so conviction is higher on direction than exact magnitude/timing.
risks_or_watch_items: HAMR mix ramp or HAMR GM may prove too aggressive; underutilization and supply-demand imbalance could delay normalization.
Flash/SSD substitution in DC could limit HDD demand and weaken the AI-related storage tailwind.
Competitor tech catch-up could reduce STX pricing power and share gain potential.
HDD price/TB could fall faster than modeled as HAMR economics are shared with customers.
AI demand timing remains uncertain; near-term AI capex is currently somewhat cannibalistic to storage spend.
Opex could ramp faster than expected, reducing EPS flow-through.

reports.title: Harnessing the Power of Heat: HDD Versus SSD - Ahead of the Curve Series
reports.headline: TD Cowen argues HAMR should extend HDD relevance in DC storage, with STX the clearest beneficiary and PT raised to $78.
reports.summary: TD Cowen lays out a bullish HDD/NL storage thesis centered on HAMR, arguing HDDs should remain the dominant DC storage medium through 2032 despite SSD gains because HAMR materially lowers HDD cost/TB and can lift OEM GMs. The report sees STX as the main beneficiary given its HAMR lead, models HDDs at ~80% of DC storage by decade-end, and views SSD adoption as constrained by cost, capex intensity, and high-capacity supply even during NAND downcycles.
important_observations: Variant view vs cons is that HDDs and SSDs remain complementary in DCs, with HDDs still storing ~80% of data by 2032.
HAMR is framed as a structural cost/margin inflection for HDDs, not just a capacity upgrade; 32TB HAMR is modeled at 30% lower cost/TB vs 20TB CMR.
AI is viewed as a net HDD demand tailwind because training datasets require cheap bulk storage, even if SSDs serve performance-sensitive workloads.
TD Cowen argues SSD share gains in NAND downcycles should be limited because hyperscalers treat trough SSD pricing as unsustainable and high-capacity SSD supply is constrained.
WDC is seen as behind STX on HAMR but not facing catastrophic share loss; Cowen expects only low/mid-single-digit share loss as WDC bridges with CMR/SMR scaling.
PSTG is viewed as a targeted beneficiary of lower NAND pricing and QLC adoption, but not a full nearline HDD replacement story.
LRCX is a read-through on rising NAND process complexity, especially as stacking/layer counts increase and deposition requirements rise.
new_data: Proprietary framework suggests HAMR can be up to 500 bps GM accretive for HDD OEMs.
Cowen estimates HAMR yields need to exceed ~40% to be GM-neutral vs CMR.
Field work indicates a major CSP could test ~25K HAMR drives in CY23 and potentially deploy up to 2M drives within 2 years if qualified.
Cowen models HDD cost/TB remaining ~4x cheaper than SSD by decade-end, or ~2x cheaper on TCO basis.
Cowen forecasts a $24B total HDD market by 2032, with NL HDD rev growing 7% CAGR from 2022.
Cowen estimates $1.5B of NAND WFE spend is needed for SSD suppliers to take 1% share from nearline HDDs.
management_insights: Seagate had shipped 32TB HAMR drives to a cloud customer for qualification in Apr-23, with rev ramp expected in early CY24.
Seagate CFO commentary cited by Cowen suggests HAMR product introductions every 18-20 months vs 10-12 months historically for PMR.
Mgmt LT GM target for STX is referenced at 30-33%; Cowen sees upside to ~34% by CY26 if HAMR ramps well.
Cowen notes WDC has improved enterprise SSD qualification execution after prior controller/firmware issues.

estimate_change_details.note: STX PT raised to $78 from $70.
Cowen frames HAMR upside as roughly $8.0/sh in earnings power; no prior EPS estimate disclosed.
Cowen models GM expansion toward 34%; prior published estimate revision not provided.

evidence_quality: Primary + analyst model work: report uses company-reported market data, disclosed product timing, and Cowen financial modeling.
Triangulated field work: multiple conclusions rely on channel checks/field work with CSPs and storage ecosystem contacts rather than solely public disclosures.
Proprietary dataset/inference: HAMR yield, GM accretion, cost/TB, and share scenarios are largely Cowen analytical constructs, not company-guided outputs.
Some datapoints are anecdotal but directionally useful, especially CSP qualification/deployment commentary on HAMR.
Weight should be moderate: thesis is detailed and cross-checked, but key conclusions depend on future HAMR yields, customer qualification, and NAND pricing behavior.
risks_or_watch_items: STX HAMR manufacturing yields and timing of full customer qualification.
Cloud/DC capex trends, since Cowen estimates ~70% of nearline HDD demand is tied to new DC builds.
Duration/severity of NAND pricing pressure and whether another sharp downcycle temporarily improves SSD competitiveness.
Ramp of 30TB+ high-capacity SSDs and whether supply broadens faster than expected.
Potential NAND technology improvements that re-accelerate cost/TB declines.
Pressure in non-nearline HDD categories, where SSD substitution risk is higher.
key_numbers: STX PT raised to $78 from $70.
HAMR could be up to 500 bps GM accretive for HDD OEMs.
HAMR yield threshold for GM neutrality estimated at ~40% vs ~85% CMR yields today.
HDDs seen at ~80% of DC storage by decade-end vs ~85-86% in 2022.
32TB HAMR modeled at 30% lower cost/TB vs 20TB CMR; 36TB HAMR ~10% lower vs 32TB HAMR.
Cowen forecasts total HDD market at $24B by 2032 with nearline HDD rev CAGR of 7%.
SSD/HDD price ratio seen near ~4x by 2032; TCO ratio below 2x by decade-end.
Nearline HDD exabyte growth modeled at ~18-20% CAGR through 2032.
STX could gain ~500 bps nearline share through CY26.
A major CSP may test ~25K HAMR drives in CY23 and potentially deploy up to 2M over 2 years.

reports.title: Moving STX to Top Pick As We Gain Greater Confidence In Cycle Lonity and Margins
reports.headline: MS moves STX to Top Pick, keeps OW, cuts PT to $129 from $133; thesis is cycle longevity + structurally higher GM despite a transitory Mar-qtr supply hit.
reports.summary: MS upgrades STX to its IT Hardware Top Pick on rising conviction that the HDD upcycle has more room to run, Mar-qtr supply issues are temporary, and Street GM/EPS remain too low as HAMR mix scales. The note cuts PT to $129 from $133 on lower NTM EPS, but argues for a June-qtr rev snap-back, continued pricing strength, tighter supply, and FY26-27 EPS upside vs cons driven by materially higher GM assumptions.
important_observations: June-qtr rev snap-back is central to the call: MS sees +11% q/q rev growth vs typical -1% seasonality once supply constraints ease.
Variant view is on margins, not just demand: MS sees FY26-27 GM at 37.8%/39.6%, well above Street, driven by HAMR mix and stronger non-HAMR pricing/profitability.
Recent industry checks suggest NL HDD demand remains robust after 6 qtrs of capacity growth, with CSP inventories still normal and lead times still ~52 weeks.
MS argues the market is overly focused on cyclical rollover/share loss risk while underappreciating STX's 24/28TB traction and HAMR-led competitiveness into C2H25/CY26.
High short interest and low expectations could amplify upside if post-earnings confidence builds around June recovery, further HAMR quals, and FY26 buybacks.
new_data: Recent industry checks were positive outside a brief early-2025 CSP digestion period.
CSP HDD inventories are described as normal/not elevated.
HDD lead times remain about 52 weeks, consistent with the prior 6 months.
Growing LTAs/build-to-order agreements are said to be improving production visibility, in some cases into 2026.
VAR survey points to accelerating storage/server demand into 2025 from enterprise OEM channels.
Asia component-supplier checks suggest better-than-seasonal Mar-qtr restocking tied to end-demand, not channel inventory build.
Updated HAMR volume/margin work now assumes HAMR is ~1% of HDD EB in FY25 and up to ~50% by FY27.
management_insights: Company disclosed on Dec. 3, 2024 that Mar-qtr HDD output would be lower than expected, with up to $200M rev impact.
Mgmt said HAMR began shipping in small volume to non-cloud customers in F2Q25.
Mgmt indicated HAMR shipments should pick up more materially around mid-CY25 as the first cloud customer ramps.
Mgmt has cited mid-20s % mass-capacity CAGR targets that predated HAMR launch and any AI tailwind.
Mgmt LT GM target referenced by MS is 30-33%, while MS believes actual normalized GM can exceed that.

estimate_change_details.note: PT cut on lower NTM EPS despite unchanged 14x multiple.
Cut reflects disclosed supply shortfall.
Lower rev base from supply issue drives the EPS reset.
Lower price/EB assumptions and Mar-qtr supply impact.
Now roughly in line with Street despite lower internal forecast.
HAMR mix lowers price/EB, offset partly by better profitability.
Still 19% above cons on higher GM assumptions.
Lower rev path reflects higher HAMR mix and lower price/EB.
Rounded; essentially flat to slightly up as GM gains offset lower rev.
Down ~70 bps from supply-driven shipment impact.
Up 190 bps on higher HAMR mix and stronger base margins.
Up 420 bps; MS sees materially stronger normalized margin structure.

evidence_quality: Primary: company-reported results, 8-K disclosure, and mgmt commentary on HAMR shipments/qualification timing.
Triangulated: analyst industry checks with CSPs, enterprise OEMs, and Asia-based component suppliers support the demand/supply view.
Survey-based: VAR survey is directionally useful for enterprise demand but lower-confidence than reported orders/shipment data.
Analyst model work: HAMR volume, pricing, and margin framework is heavily model-driven and important to the upside case.
Market/industry data: cloud capex correlations, lead times, pricing trends, and shipment trackers add support but are partly inferential.
risks_or_watch_items: Mar-qtr supply issue could persist longer than MS expects, delaying the June rebound.
WDC share gains in high-capacity NL drives remain a competitive risk until HAMR/24TB+/28TB traction broadens.
HAMR qualification/ramp timing could slip again, especially at large cloud customers.
If cloud capex or enterprise storage demand softens, the cycle-longevity thesis weakens.
HDD pricing could normalize faster than expected, pressuring GM and EPS.
AI-driven HDD demand may prove more modest or later than the thesis assumes.
key_numbers: Rating: OW; Top Pick changed from AAPL to STX.
PT: $129 vs prior $133.
STX close (Jan. 17, 2025): $97.67; implied upside ~32%.
F3Q25 rev/EPS now $2.1B/$1.65 vs prior $2.3B/$2.14.
June-qtr rev expected at $2.4B, +11% q/q vs T5Y seasonality of -1%.
FY26/FY27 EPS are 19%/34% above cons.
HAMR assumed to rise from ~1% of HDD EB in FY25 to ~50% by FY27.
FY27 HAMR volume forecast: ~11M units and ~382 EB.
Like-for-like HDD pricing expected up LSD q/q in CY25; per-EB HDD/SSD cost gap seen at 6-8x through 2028.

reports.title: Seagate Technology PLC: The Dawn of The Zettabyte Age
reports.headline: BTIG argues STX is underappreciated as a core beneficiary of secular data growth, with HDD remaining the economic capacity tier for cloud and edge-generated data; Maintain Buy, $55 PT.
reports.summary: BTIG reiterates Buy on STX and frames the stock as a mispriced beneficiary of the coming "zettabyte age," arguing that secular data creation, cloud build-outs, AI/ML, and edge computing should sustain HDD bit demand well beyond what unit declines imply. The note’s core thesis is that while flash dominates performance use cases, HDD remains the only economical medium for large-scale capacity storage, supporting STX’s mix shift toward higher-margin nearline enterprise and improved EPS power.
important_observations: Core debate is not whether data grows, but whether investors are underestimating the value of the low-cost storage layer required to retain that data at scale.
BTIG argues counting HDD units misses the thesis; bit/EB growth and mix shift to high-capacity enterprise drives are more relevant than spindle declines.
Nearline enterprise is already becoming the economic engine of STX, with materially better growth, mix, and profit profile than legacy PC/client HDD.

The note frames STX as a variant-perception idea: bearish HDD views are inconsistent with bullish secular data-growth views unless memory capex rises massively or data-retention assumptions prove too optimistic.
BTIG sees cloud and intelligent-edge buildouts as additive, not cannibalistic, with edge inference increasing the need for centralized cloud storage for training, retention, and metadata.
Valuation argument is that the market is still pricing STX on cyclical/legacy HDD concerns rather than as a secular data infrastructure beneficiary.
new_data: IDC Data Age 2025 forecast: global datasphere to reach 163 ZB by 2025 vs 16.1 ZB in 2016.
IDC forecast: hypercritical data to grow at 54% CAGR vs 30% for total data.
IDC forecast: embedded data to reach 20% of all data created by 2020.
IDC forecast: IoT data to represent 95% of real-time data by 2025.
IDC forecast: analyzed data to reach 5.2 ZB by 2024; data interacting with cognitive systems to reach 1.4 ZB by 2025.
BTIG cloud capex forecast for major platforms rises to $66.7B in FY18E from $50.9B in FY16A.
BTIG shows flash remains at a large $/GB premium to enterprise HDD through FY25E despite narrowing.
IDC/BTIG suggest HDD still represents roughly 50% of storage bits shipped in 2025, with enterprise HDD >80% of enterprise HDD/SSD mix.
BTIG base-case storage model assumes 18% combined data growth and ~1.8 ZB HDD demand by 2025.
management_insights: Mgmt has publicly discussed a $30B-$40B HDD opportunity based on 1.5-2.0 ZB of HDD storage demand.
Company guidance for CY17 EPS of at least $4.50 was well above prior market expectations and used by BTIG as evidence that the mix/cost reset is working.
BTIG cites public signs of healthier factory utilization, stable pricing, post-flood-high GM, and continued mix shift away from PC exposure.
Mgmt appears focused on portfolio realignment toward high-capacity cloud/enterprise, surveillance, gaming, DVR, and other higher-capacity use cases.
estimate_changes: No explicit analyst rev/EPS/PT revision versus BTIG’s prior note is disclosed; BTIG maintains Buy and $55 PT.
The note highlights stronger company outlook vs prior market expectations, especially CY17 EPS guide of at least $4.50 vs prior consensus near $3.80.
evidence_quality: Primary/triangulated: uses IDC's March 2017 Data Age 2025 study, company-reported results/guidance, and BTIG/FactSet market data.
Primary company evidence: references Seagate-reported nearline EB growth, rev mix, margin trends, and company outlook.
Mostly analyst model work/inference: long-dated HDD/NAND bit-growth, revenue, and EPS power scenarios are BTIG framework assumptions rather than company guidance.
Some third-party industry support: Gartner, Google white paper, silicon supply commentary, and cloud capex datasets help triangulate storage-demand and supply constraints.
Weight should be moderate: strong for secular framing and current mix/profit trends, lower for precise 2020-2025 TAM, bit-share, and valuation outcomes.
risks_or_watch_items: CSP demand remains lumpy with limited NT visibility despite broader customer diversification.
If NAND cost declines or memory capex ramp faster than BTIG assumes, HDD economics and long-term share could weaken.
Retention assumptions matter: if more real-time/IoT data is discarded rather than stored, capacity demand could underwhelm.
Legacy PC/client HDD declines still pressure consolidated units and can obscure improvement in higher-value segments.
Execution risk remains around sustaining double-digit capacity growth, stable pricing, and continued enterprise mix gains.
key_numbers: STX rating/PT: Buy, $55 PT.
STX share price: $45.30; implied upside 21.4%.
Dividend yield: 5.6%; market cap: $13.5B.
BTIG FY17E sales/EPS: $11.168B / $4.56.
BTIG FY18E sales/EPS: $11.265B / $4.53.
PT methodology: 12x run-rate EPS of about $4.50.
Seagate high-capacity enterprise EB growth: 63% in CY16.
High-capacity enterprise rev: about $2.7B in CY16; rev mix expanded from 21% to 32%.
Gross margin cited at 32% vs FY16 of 25%.
IDC datasphere forecast: 163 ZB by 2025 vs 16.1 ZB in 2016.
IDC says 19 ZB of storage must ship in aggregate over the next 8 years to keep up with demand.
BTIG base case: about 1.0 ZB HDD demand in 2020; STX at 40% share implies about $9B HDD rev on 130M units at $70 ASP.
BTIG earnings-power framework: FY2019 EPS $4-$6 and FY2020 EPS $5-$7 under favorable capacity scenarios.

reports.title: CQ1'16 Earnings Preview: ANET, INFN, SNDK, STX, WDC Plus HDD / SSD Forecast Update and Raising STX PT to $27
reports.headline: Citi previews CQ1/FQ3 storage and networking prints, cuts LT HDD unit ests, lifts SSD outlook, trims WDC FY17/FY18 EPS, and raises STX PT to $27.
reports.summary: Citi’s 7-Apr-2016 preview is a multi-name storage/networking note with the key sector call being a lower 2017-2018 HDD unit outlook and higher SSD adoption assumptions. For WDC, Citi sees limited downside into FQ3'16 with rev/EPS roughly in line, but flows the weaker HDD / faster SSD mix into its model and trims FY17/FY18 EPS to $6.32/$6.66 from $6.40/$6.95. Citi still prefers WDC over STX on better positioning for SSD migration and the pending SNDK deal, while separately raising STX’s PT to $27 on multiple expansion despite maintaining a Sell.
important_observations: Citi’s main variant view is that the HDD decline extends into 2017-2018 as soft PC demand and faster SSD migration pressure the storage complex more than prior assumptions.
For WDC, downside into the quarter is framed as limited because Citi is using a conservative ~100M drive TAM vs industry at 103-104M, reducing the risk of a material near-term miss.
Citi remains structurally more positive on WDC than STX because WDC is viewed as better levered to SSD migration and captive NAND via the pending SNDK acquisition.
Enterprise storage mix is shifting faster toward SSDs, which Citi argues strengthens the strategic logic of the WDC-SNDK merger.
ANET’s 2H setup is tied to Tomahawk-based upgrades and 100Gb spine buildouts, while INFN’s thesis leans on Metro/DCI growth offset by telco capex seasonality and R&D drag.
SNDK fundamentals appear less important than deal timing, with the stock viewed as trading mainly on the pending WDC acquisition and remaining MOFCOM approval.
new_data: HDD unit ests cut to -5.4% growth in 2017 and -4.0% in 2018 from -0.3% and -1.8%; 2016 HDD decline unchanged at -12%.
Client SSD attach implied at 55% of total PC shipments exiting 2018 vs 22% currently.
Total HDD units revised to 392.7M in 2017 from 414.0M and 377.1M in 2018 from 406.5M.
Enterprise HDD units revised to 63.5M in 2017 from 68.2M and 61.8M in 2018 from 68.2M.
WDC FQ3'16 preview: rev $2.856B, EPS $1.28, 42.1M HDD units, ASP $62.
STX FQ3'16 preview: rev $2.696B, EPS $0.60, 40.8M HDD units, ASP $60.
SNDK CQ1'16 preview: rev $1.231B, EPS $0.57, with bits shipped down 10% q/q and ASPs down 8% q/q.
management_insights: SNDK is expected to issue results and written commentary without a conference call as the WDC merger approaches close.
Citi expects WDC to guide FQ4'16 toward a slightly >100M HDD TAM and seasonal rev recovery to $2.9B-$3.0B.
Citi expects ANET commentary to stay constructive on Webscale upgrades and 100Gb spine deployments into 2H16.

estimate_change_details.note: Citi trimmed WDC FY17 EPS on updated HDD/SSD industry assumptions.
Citi trimmed WDC FY18 EPS on updated HDD/SSD industry assumptions.
PT raised on sector multiple expansion and more balanced NT risk.
Citi lowered FY17 EPS to reflect HDD/SSD model updates.
Citi lowered FY18 EPS to reflect HDD/SSD model updates.

evidence_quality: Primary/analyst model work: detailed Citi earnings previews, company guidance ranges, and Citi vs Street bridges for ANET/INFN/SNDK/STX/WDC.
Market/industry data: HDD and SSD unit forecasts appear built from Trendfocus, FactSet, and Citi’s storage model; useful for sector framing but still model-driven.
Triangulated channel/adjacent data: note cites Webscale capex, NA telco capex, supplier commentary, and Taiwan semi supply-chain read-throughs rather than new company-reported datapoints.
Limited primary mgmt evidence: this is largely a pre-earnings inference note, not a post-call readout or mgmt mtg summary, so conviction on quarter specifics should be weighted accordingly.
risks_or_watch_items: PC demand could stay weaker for longer, pressuring HDD TAM and WDC/STX estimates further.
Faster-than-modeled SSD migration remains the key structural risk to HDD-centric names.
WDC-SNDK close timing still depends on final MOFCOM approval.
STX may see NT GM upside, but Citi still sees LT strategy and possible disruptive M&A as overhangs.
WDC’s near-term print is sensitive to HDD units/ASP around a ~100M industry TAM assumption.
INFN demand outlook still depends on telco capex seasonality and whether Metro/DCI offsets hold.
key_numbers: WDC FQ3'16 est: rev $2.856B, EPS $1.28, GM 28.8%, OM 11.3%, HDD units 42.1M, ASP $62.
WDC FY17/FY18 EPS revised to $6.32/$6.66 from $6.40/$6.95.
STX PT raised to $27 from $23; FY17/FY18 EPS cut to $3.56/$3.38 from $3.62/$3.46.
2017/2018 total HDD units cut to 392.7M/377.1M from 414.0M/406.5M.
2016/2017/2018 SSD unit growth fcst raised to 28.4%/15.7%/13.2%.
Client SSD attach seen at 55% of PC shipments by end-2018 vs 22% currently.
SNDK CQ1'16 est: rev $1.231B, EPS $0.57; deal close could come as early as mid-Q2 pending MOFCOM.
ANET Q1'16 est: rev $237M, EPS $0.61; INFN Q1'16 est: rev $245M, EPS $0.17.

reports.title: Ares Management Corp. (ARES): ARCC read-across: Slower deployment and modest pick up in non-accruals
reports.headline: GS says ARCC's 1Q26 print points to slower direct-lending deployment, muted 2Q activity, and modestly worse credit metrics, with implications for ARES fees and sentiment.
reports.summary: Goldman reiterates Buy on ARES and uses ARCC's 1Q26 results as a read-across for ARES' credit business: net deployment slowed sharply, April activity stayed muted, and non-accruals ticked up modestly, reinforcing a softer NT origination backdrop. The note argues scaled platforms like ARES should benefit when activity normalizes given roughly $100bn of available credit capital, but investors are likely to stay focused on whether weaker deployment and rising non-accruals signal broader credit-performance divergence across managers.
important_observations: Net new commitments fell sharply even though new-money spreads stayed broadly stable, implying the bottleneck is activity/vol rather than pricing alone.
April commitment pace remained soft and backlog declined, suggesting 2Q deployment may stay muted absent a market reopening.
Non-accruals increased but remain within historical ranges, keeping focus on whether underlying credit trends are starting to bifurcate across managers.
Portfolio EBITDA weakened q/q while leverage edged up, a combination that bears watching if private-credit fundamentals soften further.
ARCC management fees were modestly ahead of GS's ARES model, providing a small offset to softer deployment trends.
new_data: 1Q26 gross new commitments were $3,246mn vs $5,825mn in 4Q25 and $3,453mn y/y.
1Q26 net new commitments slowed to $70mn vs $1,164mn in 4Q25 and $596mn y/y.
1Q26 net fundings were $277mn vs $515mn in 4Q25 and $243mn y/y.
Debt-investment yield on fundings was 9.2% in 1Q26 vs 9.9% in 1Q25; implied spread was ~550bps on quarter-end SOFR.
Apr MTD through Apr 23 gross commitments were ~$198mn vs exits/repayments of ~$13mn; backlog was ~$1.8bn vs $2.2bn at end-Jan.
Non-accruals rose 30bps q/q to 2.1% of cost; fair-value non-accruals were 1.2%, flat q/q.
Portfolio weighted-average EBITDA fell 5% q/q to $291mn; net lev rose to 5.7x from 5.6x.
Mgmt fees were $111mn, ~3% above GS's published ARES model and +9% y/y.
management_insights: Mgmt flagged muted activity so far in 2Q, with commitments below prior-year April levels.
ARCC disclosed only sub-$200mn of commitments through Apr 23, underscoring a slower origination environment.
GS expects ARES can accelerate deployment once markets stabilize because of its scale and ~$100bn of available capital.
Credit performance is likely to stay in focus as non-accrual trends begin to diverge by manager.

estimate_change_details.note: PT cut per GS target-price history table; Buy rating reiterated.

evidence_quality: Primary: based mainly on ARCC's company-reported 1Q26 results, earnings release, slide deck, and 10-Q datapoints.
Primary: includes company-disclosed Apr MTD origination/backlog metrics, which are timely and directly relevant for NT read-across.
Triangulated but indirect: conclusions for ARES rely on ARCC as a fee/contribution proxy rather than direct ARES segment reporting.
Mostly analyst inference for broader alt-manager implications, especially around deployment normalization and manager-by-manager credit divergence.
risks_or_watch_items: Wider credit spreads or ongoing vol could keep deployment and fee growth muted.
Further increases in non-accruals could pressure sentiment and raise concern on asset quality.
Backlog erosion and weak Apr commitments raise risk that 2Q originations stay below recent run-rate.
Realization timing remains a downside risk per GS.
Watch whether portfolio EBITDA pressure and slightly higher lev become a broader trend.
key_numbers: ARES rating/PT: Buy, $131 PT; price $112.18; implied upside 16.8%.
ARCC contributes 30%+ of ARES Credit segment mgmt fees incl. Part I fees.
ARES available credit capital: ~$100bn.
1Q26 gross commitments: $3,246mn; net new commitments: $70mn; net fundings: $277mn.
Apr 23 MTD gross commitments: ~$198mn; exits/repayments: ~$13mn; backlog: ~$1.8bn.
Yield on funded debt investments: 9.2% in 1Q26; Apr funded yield: 9.1%; exited/repaid yield: 9.7%.
Non-accruals: 2.1% of cost vs 1.8% in 4Q25; 1.2% of fair value.
Interest coverage: 2.2x; weighted-average portfolio EBITDA: $291mn; net lev: 5.7x.
Mgmt fees: $111mn; Part I fees: $84mn vs Visible Alpha cons $88mn.
